title: E-commerce Price Monitoring
cardTeaser: Stopped checking prices manually — now gets Slack alerts before breakfast.
summary: A Texas e-commerce brand was getting undercut overnight with no visibility until customers compared prices on Google.
challenge: Ops spent 3 hours every morning copying competitor prices into Excel and still missed mid-day changes.
solution: Nightly scraper across 12 stores with Slack alerts when any price drops more than 5%.
results: Reaction time went from days to hours. Paid for itself in week one.
context: A Texas-based Shopify brand selling home goods competed against a dozen similar stores. Pricing changed daily — sometimes hourly during sales seasons — and their ops lead spent every morning manually checking competitor sites.
By the time spreadsheets were updated, prices had already shifted again. They were reacting days late, losing margin on flash sales and missing chances to undercut rivals on slow movers.
challengeParagraphs: Manual tracking could not scale across 12 stores × 200 SKUs. Human error meant wrong prices in decision decks.
solutionParagraphs: Automated nightly scrape with intraday re-runs during Black Friday. Alerts include old price, new price, and direct link.
resultsParagraphs: Reaction time collapsed from days to under an hour. Team repriced strategically and recovered margin on two key categories.

painPoints.label: 3+ hours every morning on manual price checks
Margin leaks from reacting days too late

approach: Scoped 12 competitor storefronts — confirmed public product pages only.
Mapped SKU fields needed: price, compare-at, stock badge, title hash.
Built Python scrapers with rotating residential proxies.
Normalized data into PostgreSQL with change-detection triggers.
Slack webhook alerts when any SKU drops more than 5%.
Daily CSV export for merchandising team legacy workflow.
Dashboard for ops lead — filter by category, sort by biggest delta.
Two-week parallel run vs manual checks to validate accuracy.
Documented runbook for adding new competitors.
Handoff: cron on Render, env vars, on-call fix SLA.

timeline.detail: Legal review, target list, field mapping, alert rules.
Scrapers, database, diff engine, Slack integration.
Parallel manual comparison, tuning, production deploy.

outcomes.label: Weekly time saved
outcomes.detail: Ops lead redirected to strategy work.

outcomes.label: ROI
outcomes.detail: Tool paid for itself after first prevented undercut.

deepSections.title: Background & context
paragraphs: A Texas-based Shopify brand selling home goods competed against a dozen similar stores. Pricing changed daily — sometimes hourly during sales seasons — and their ops lead spent every morning manually checking competitor sites.
By the time spreadsheets were updated, prices had already shifted again. They were reacting days late, losing margin on flash sales and missing chances to undercut rivals on slow movers.

paragraphs: Manual tracking could not scale across 12 stores × 200 SKUs. Human error meant wrong prices in decision decks.

deepSections.title: Outcomes & business impact
paragraphs: Reaction time collapsed from days to under an hour. Team repriced strategically and recovered margin on two key categories.

title: Restaurant Website Redesign
cardTeaser: PDF menu and no click-to-call → 40% more phone orders in month one.
summary: A family Italian restaurant had a 2016 site that fell apart on phones where 80% of searches happen.
challenge: Menu was a PDF. Hours buried in footer. Customers said the site looked closed.
solution: Mobile-first rebuild with scrollable menu, sticky call button, and sub-2s load on 4G.
results: Phone orders jumped 40%. Three Google reviews praised the easy new site.
context: A family-run Italian restaurant in Ohio had built a loyal local following over 15 years — but their digital front door told a different story. The website was built in 2016, never updated, and on mobile it looked abandoned.

The owner knew most new customers found them on Google from their phones. Yet the menu was a PDF that required pinch-zoom, hours were buried in the footer, and there was no obvious way to call or order. Staff reported hearing "I thought you were closed" several times a week.
They did not need a fancy rebrand — they needed a site that matched the warmth of the dining room and made calling or visiting effortless on a 5-inch screen.
challengeParagraphs: The previous site was a generic WordPress theme with a stock photo of pasta that did not match their actual dishes. On mobile the navigation collapsed into a hamburger menu that hid the phone number entirely.
The PDF menu downloaded instead of displaying inline — many customers on cellular data never opened it. Competitors two blocks away had modern sites with online ordering widgets.
solutionParagraphs: We rebuilt from scratch with a mobile-first layout: hero showing today's hours, one-tap call button fixed at the bottom, and a menu organized by category with real dish photos the owner provided.
We added structured data for local SEO, compressed all images to WebP, and wrote copy in plain language — no "culinary journey" fluff, just what customers need to decide to visit tonight.
resultsParagraphs: Phone orders from mobile increased 40% in the first month — the owner attributed this directly to the sticky call button and clearer hours.
Staff stopped answering "are you open?" questions as often because hours and holiday closures were visible immediately. The project paid for itself within six weeks of additional cover turns.

painPoints.label: 80% of searches on mobile — site unusable there
Menu locked inside a PDF customers hated
Outdated design made the restaurant look closed
No click-to-call — customers gave up and picked another spot

approach: Discovery call — walked through the old site on real phones and listed every friction point.
Content audit — collected menu items, hours, photos, and brand colors from the owner.
Mobile-first wireframes — sticky call button, scrollable menu, hero with same-day hours.
Design mockups — warm photography, readable typography, trust cues (years open, neighborhood).
Development — Astro + Tailwind, image optimization, local SEO basics baked in.
Menu build — categorized dishes with prices, dietary tags, no PDF anywhere.
Speed pass — compressed images, lazy loading, sub-2s target on 4G.
QA on 6 real devices — iPhone SE, Android mid-range, iPad, slow 3G throttling.
Launch + handoff — domain DNS, SSL, simple guide to update specials via spreadsheet.
Week-one check-in — Google Analytics review and small copy tweaks from staff feedback.

timeline.detail: Site audit, competitor scan, content collection, signed-off sitemap.
Mockups for home, menu, contact. Two revision rounds with owner.
Development, menu entry, forms, analytics, mobile QA.
DNS cutover, staff training, post-launch monitoring.

outcomes.label: New Google reviews
outcomes.detail: Mentioned the easy new website specifically.

outcomes.label: Mobile load time
outcomes.detail: Down from 8+ seconds on old WordPress theme.

outcomes.label: PDF menus
outcomes.detail: Fully native scrollable menu — staff can update specials.

deepSections.title: Page 1 — Where they started
paragraphs: When we first opened the site on an iPhone SE, the owner winced. The hero image was pixelated, the phone number was missing above the fold, and the only menu link downloaded a 4MB PDF.

Google Search Console showed 82% mobile traffic but bounce rate on mobile was 71% — nearly double desktop. Customers who did call often said they struggled to find hours or thought the business might be closed.
The owner had tried a cheap template refresh in 2019 but never maintained it. Staff still printed paper menus for anyone who asked because they knew the website failed on phones.

deepSections.title: Page 2 — What we changed
paragraphs: We rebuilt the information architecture around three jobs: confirm you are open, show the menu, make calling easy. Everything else moved down or off the homepage.

Photography came from the owner’s phone — real dishes, real dining room — compressed to WebP with responsive sizes. We removed WordPress entirely in favour of a static Astro build hosted on their existing domain.
Local SEO was part of delivery: Google Business Profile hours synced, schema markup for Restaurant, and page titles rewritten for “Italian restaurant + city” searches instead of generic taglines.

deepSections.title: Page 3 — Results & lessons
paragraphs: Within 30 days, call tracking showed a 40% lift in phone orders originating from mobile. Three new Google reviews explicitly mentioned how easy the site was to use — the first time that happened.
Load time dropped from 8.2s to 2.1s on throttled 4G. The owner updates weekly specials through a simple shared spreadsheet we wired to the menu — no developer needed for routine changes.
The biggest lesson: for local restaurants, the website is not marketing fluff — it is the front door. Speed, hours, and one-tap call matter more than animation or trendy design.

title: Lead Generation Bot
cardTeaser: Leads waited 4 hours for a reply. Bot responds in 90 seconds, 24/7.
summary: A 6-person agency lost warm leads after 6 PM when nobody was checking the inbox.
challenge: Generic chat widget dumped emails into a spreadsheet checked only in the morning.
solution: Telegram bot with 4 qualifying questions, lead scoring, and HubSpot push with full transcript.
results: Response time: 4 hours → 90 seconds. Qualified conversions up 28% in Q1.
context: A London marketing agency lost warm leads after 6 PM. Their chat widget emailed a spreadsheet nobody checked until morning.
challengeParagraphs: Generic widget created noise, not pipeline. Sales checked leads once daily.
solutionParagraphs: Structured bot conversation with scoring and instant CRM push.
resultsParagraphs: Response time 4h → 90s. Sales focuses on warm, scored leads only.
approach: Mapped 4 qualifying questions from sales playbook.
Built Telegram bot with branching logic and lead scoring.
HubSpot integration with full chat transcript.
Escalation to human for high-value leads.
Weekly report on drop-off points.

paragraphs: A London marketing agency lost warm leads after 6 PM. Their chat widget emailed a spreadsheet nobody checked until morning.

paragraphs: Generic widget created noise, not pipeline. Sales checked leads once daily.

deepSections.title: Our approach & delivery
paragraphs: Structured bot conversation with scoring and instant CRM push.

deepSections.title: Outcomes & business impact
paragraphs: Response time 4h → 90s. Sales focuses on warm, scored leads only.

title: Offline-First Mobile App
cardTeaser: Technicians in dead zones. App works offline, syncs when signal returns.
summary: Field techs lost job data when connectivity dropped — they went back to paper forms.
challenge: Existing app crashed without internet. Office staff re-typed everything.
solution: Offline-first Android with SQLite, photos, signatures, and background sync.
results: Zero lost jobs after launch. 30% more jobs completed per day.
context: Field technicians in rural India lost job data when connectivity dropped — reverted to paper, office re-typed everything.
resultsParagraphs: Zero lost jobs after launch. Paper forms eliminated.
approach: SQLite local store for jobs, photos, signatures.
Background sync queue when online.
Conflict resolution rules documented.
Offline maps cached per region.

title: Dental Clinic Modernization
cardTeaser: Old site looked sketchy. Online bookings = 35% of new patients.
summary: Decent local reputation but a 2009-looking site made new patients hesitate.
challenge: No online booking. Missing doctor profiles. Insurance info unreadable.
solution: Trust-first design with doctor photos, insurance logos, and integrated booking.
results: 35% of new patients book online. First time patients complimented the site.
context: A dental clinic in New Jersey had excellent chair-side reputation but a website that looked like 2009 — stock clipart, tiny text, no mobile booking.
New patients googling "dentist near me" bounced within seconds. Front desk said callers often asked "is this the right office?" because the site did not match the modern practice inside.
challengeParagraphs: Competing clinics had modern sites with instant booking. Theirs felt like a placeholder and hurt first impressions on intake calls.
solutionParagraphs: Trust-first redesign with real team photography, insurance clarity, and embedded scheduling. Every page answers "why choose us" in 5 seconds.
resultsParagraphs: 35% of new patients now book online. Front desk handles fewer routine scheduling calls and more complex care questions.

painPoints.label: Site credibility did not match in-office experience
No online booking — phone tag for appointments
Doctor profiles missing — patients could not choose

approach: Patient journey mapping — from Google search to booked appointment.
Photography direction — real team photos, not stock smiles.
Trust blocks — insurance logos, credentials, before/after gallery (with consent).
Service pages per treatment with plain-English explanations.
Integrated booking widget with availability sync.
HIPAA-aware contact forms — no PHI in email subjects.
Speed + accessibility pass — WCAG AA contrast targets.
Staff training video — 8 minutes on updating hours and closures.

timeline.detail: Competitor audit, patient interview notes, sitemap.
Trust-first UI, mobile prototypes, content writing.

outcomes.label: New patients online
outcomes.detail: Book without calling — first time ever.

deepSections.title: Background & context
paragraphs: A dental clinic in New Jersey had excellent chair-side reputation but a website that looked like 2009 — stock clipart, tiny text, no mobile booking.
New patients googling "dentist near me" bounced within seconds. Front desk said callers often asked "is this the right office?" because the site did not match the modern practice inside.

title: Amazon Product Scraper

challenge: Checking 50 products/day by hand — data stale before finishing a category.
solution: Custom scraper with proxy rotation pulling price, rating, BSR daily into PostgreSQL.
results: 200K+ products in 3 weeks. Launched products now top-10 in category.
context: FBA seller needed competitor data across 15 categories — checking 50 products/day by hand.
solutionParagraphs: Automated pipeline with daily BSR and price history.
resultsParagraphs: 200K+ products in three weeks. Data-driven launch strategy.

painPoints.label: Stale data before finishing one category

approach: Proxy rotation pool for Amazon requests.

Export API for existing tools.

title: Real Estate Listing Aggregator
cardTeaser: Checked 6 listing sites daily. One dashboard + instant alerts for new deals.
summary: Investor checked 6 portals every morning — good deals gone by the time he found them.
challenge: 2 hours daily tab-hopping. Listings often under offer before he saw them.
solution: Scrapers every 2 hours, unified dashboard, SMS when criteria match.
results: 4 below-market deals in month 1 that he would have missed manually.
context: Toronto investor checked 6 listing portals every morning — deals gone before he saw them.
challengeParagraphs: Speed was the competitive edge — manual process too slow.
resultsParagraphs: Four below-market acquisitions in first month.
approach: Scrapers every 2 hours across portals.
Unified dashboard with filters.
SMS alerts when criteria match.
Historical price chart per listing.
